title: Pruning: Trimming the Branches
concept: Pruning is the process of cutting back branches that do not help categorize new data, turning a complex, overfitted tree into a simpler, more robust model.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does an unrestricted decision tree overfit so easily?

options.text: It keeps splitting until it memorizes the exact details and random quirks of the training data.
It stops asking questions after the very first split.
It is forced to use curves instead of straight boundary questions.

exercises.explain: Correct! Without limits, a decision tree will split repeatedly until it has memorized the training points, rather than finding patterns that work generally.

cards.title: The Overgrown Tree
cards.body: If you let an apple tree grow completely wild, it will sprout hundreds of tiny, weak branches and twigs. These extra twigs steal nutrients and sun, making the tree messy and causing it to grow smaller, sour apples. To keep the tree healthy and productive, a gardener cuts back the useless twigs.

cards.title: What is Pruning?
cards.body: Pruning is a technique where we remove (cut back) branches from a decision tree that add complexity without adding useful, generalizable information. This simplifies the tree and helps it make better decisions on new data.

cards.title: Editing an Essay
cards.body: Think of pruning like editing a rough draft. When you write a first draft, you include every single detail, even irrelevant ones. Editing means cutting out the filler and small tangents so the reader can understand the main points clearly. Pruning does the exact same thing to a tree's questions!

cards.title: The Clean Spam Filter
cards.body: A spam detection tree has a branch asking: 'Does the email contain "buy now"?' — this is a great rule. But it also has a sub-question: 'Was the email sent at exactly 3:14 AM on a Tuesday?' This rule is too specific and only happened by chance. Pruning cuts off that sub-question, leaving just the strong 'buy now' rule.

cards.title: Losing Training Accuracy is Good
cards.body: You might think cutting branches is bad because it makes the tree less accurate on the training data. In fact, while it might slightly lower accuracy on the training data, it makes the model much more accurate on new, unseen data!

cards.title: Unpruned vs. Pruned Decisions
cards.body: How pruning changes the rules of a decision tree:

left.text:
If price > $100 AND customer is wearing blue shoes AND it is Tuesday -> High Risk.
Too many irrelevant rules.

right.text:
If price > $100 -> High Risk.
Simplified down to the only factor that actually matters.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary action taken when pruning a decision tree?

options.text: Removing overly specific branches that do not help classify new data.
Adding new questions to make the tree deeper and more complex.
Combining multiple decision trees into a single linear regression line.
Replacing the tree's questions with mathematical formulas.

exercises.explain: Correct! Pruning removes the complex, hyper-specific branches to keep the model focused on general patterns.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Pruning a decision tree can sometimes lower its accuracy on the training set while raising its accuracy on the test set.
exercises.explain: True! By cutting out hyper-specific rules, the tree might misclassify a few training outliers, but it will perform much better on new, unseen data.

exercises.sentence: The process of cutting away weak, unnecessary branches from a decision tree is called ___.
exercises.explain: Pruning keeps decision trees small, clean, and accurate on new data.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios shows a model being pruned?

options.text: An engineer removes a sub-question about the buyer's favorite color from a home valuation tree because it doesn't affect price.
An engineer adds a sub-question about the buyer's birth month to make the tree more precise.
An engineer collects twice as much data to train a deeper tree.

exercises.explain: Correct! Removing the irrelevant sub-question ('favorite color') is an example of pruning the tree to make it simpler and more general.

exercises.prompt: A bank's loan approval tree has a branch that says: 'If income > $50k AND favorite color is green -> Approve.' How should a machine learning engineer prune this branch, and why?

options.text: Remove the 'favorite color is green' check, because favorite color is irrelevant to loan payback and won't help categorize new customers.
Keep the green color check because the training data showed green-loving customers always paid back loans.
Delete the entire branch including the income check, so the tree has no rules at all.
Add a new question checking if their second favorite color is blue to double-check.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Favorite color is an outlier coincidence in the training data. Pruning that branch makes the rule generalizable: 'If income > $50k -> Approve.' Next, let's learn how to prevent these deep branches from forming in the first place!
